title: Introduction to Art Appreciation
content:
This open textbook provides a comprehensive introduction to the visual arts, covering the elements and principles of design, major art movements from antiquity through the contemporary period, and diverse cultural perspectives on artistic expression. Students will learn to analyze and interpret works of art using formal, contextual, and comparative methods.

The resource includes high-resolution images of key works, interactive timelines of art history, and guided analysis exercises. Each chapter concludes with discussion prompts and portfolio-building activities designed for introductory-level college courses.

Topics covered include drawing, painting, sculpture, architecture, photography, and digital media. The textbook emphasizes visual literacy and critical thinking as foundational skills for understanding art in everyday life and across cultures.
subject: Art

title: Writing First: Composition for College Students
content:
Writing First is an open educational resource designed for first-year composition courses. It guides students through the writing process from brainstorming and outlining to drafting, revising, and editing. The textbook covers rhetorical analysis, argumentation, research-based writing, and proper citation practices using MLA and APA formats.

Each chapter includes sample student essays, peer review worksheets, and revision checklists. The resource is structured around a workshop model where students develop their writing through iterative feedback and reflection.

Additional sections address grammar and mechanics, academic integrity, and strategies for writing across disciplines. The textbook is suitable for ENGL 1101 and equivalent first-semester composition courses at two-year and four-year institutions.
subject: English Composition

title: Literature and Composition: Reading, Writing, Thinking
content:
This course resource supports ENGL 1102 by integrating literary analysis with composition skills. Students read short fiction, poetry, drama, and creative nonfiction while developing critical essays that engage with literary texts. The syllabus emphasizes close reading, thesis development, and evidence-based argumentation.

The resource includes curated reading lists featuring diverse authors and genres, writing assignment sequences that build from response papers to full literary analysis essays, and rubrics aligned with Georgia Guided Pathways standards.

Students also engage with secondary sources and literary criticism, learning to synthesize multiple perspectives in their writing. The course prepares students for upper-division literature and writing-intensive courses across the curriculum.
subject: English Literature

title: US History to 1877: An Open Resource
content:
This open textbook covers American history from Indigenous civilizations through Reconstruction. Topics include European colonization, the American Revolution, the Constitution, westward expansion, slavery and abolition, the Civil War, and early Reconstruction. Primary source documents are embedded throughout each chapter.

The resource uses a thematic approach alongside chronological narrative, highlighting the experiences of diverse groups including Native Americans, enslaved Africans, women, and immigrants. Each chapter includes maps, timelines, and guided document analysis activities.

Designed for HIST 2111 and equivalent survey courses, this textbook meets Quality Matters standards for online and hybrid delivery. Instructor supplements include test banks, lecture slides, and discussion prompts.
subject: US History

title: United States History Since 1877
content:
This course resource covers the major political, social, economic, and cultural developments in the United States from the end of Reconstruction to the present. Topics include industrialization, the Progressive Era, World War I, the Great Depression, World War II, the Civil Rights Movement, the Cold War, and contemporary America.

The syllabus integrates primary source analysis with textbook readings, requiring students to evaluate historical arguments and construct evidence-based interpretations. Assignments include document-based essays, a research project, and participation in structured class discussions.

This resource is aligned with the Georgia Gwinnett College HIST 2112 learning outcomes and supports the Affordable Learning Georgia initiative by replacing commercial textbooks with open and library-licensed materials.
subject: US History

title: Introduction to Computer Science and Information Technology
content:
This open textbook introduces foundational concepts in computer science and information technology. Topics include hardware and software fundamentals, operating systems, networking basics, cybersecurity principles, data representation, and an introduction to programming logic using pseudocode and Python.

The resource is designed for non-majors and first-year IT students, requiring no prior programming experience. Each chapter includes hands-on lab exercises, self-assessment quizzes, and real-world case studies that connect computing concepts to everyday technology use.

Additional modules cover digital citizenship, ethical issues in technology, cloud computing, and emerging trends such as artificial intelligence and the Internet of Things. The textbook supports ITEC 1001 and equivalent introductory computing courses.
subject: Information Technology

title: Principles of Biology I: Molecules and Cells
content:
This open textbook and lab manual covers introductory biology with emphasis on molecular and cellular biology. Topics include the chemistry of life, cell structure and function, cellular respiration, photosynthesis, DNA replication, gene expression, and an introduction to genetics and evolution.

The lab component includes twelve guided experiments covering microscopy, enzyme kinetics, cell division, DNA extraction, and gel electrophoresis. Each lab includes pre-lab questions, detailed procedures, data collection templates, and post-lab analysis questions.

Designed for BIOL 1101K and equivalent courses, this resource meets the learning outcomes for science majors and pre-health students. The textbook integrates current research examples and emphasizes scientific literacy and experimental design skills.
subject: Biology

title: Principles of Biology II: Organisms and Ecosystems
content:
This course resource covers the second semester of introductory biology, focusing on organismal biology, ecology, and evolution. Topics include plant and animal physiology, biodiversity, taxonomy, population ecology, community interactions, ecosystem dynamics, and conservation biology.

The syllabus integrates lecture content with field-based and laboratory activities. Lab exercises include plant anatomy dissections, invertebrate and vertebrate comparative anatomy, ecological sampling methods, and a semester-long biodiversity monitoring project.

Aligned with Georgia Gwinnett College BIOL 1102 learning outcomes, this resource supports the transition from cellular to organismal and ecological perspectives. The course uses a combination of open textbook chapters and curated open-access journal articles.
subject: Biology

title: GGC English Composition Syllabus Collection
content:
This collection of syllabi from Georgia Gwinnett College's ENGL 1101 courses documents the range of approaches used to teach first-year composition. The collection includes syllabi emphasizing rhetorical analysis, multimodal composition, community-engaged writing, and writing about social issues.

Each syllabus includes course learning outcomes aligned with the University System of Georgia's core curriculum requirements, assignment sequences, grading criteria, and required readings. Many syllabi incorporate open educational resources as primary or supplementary texts.

The collection serves as a resource for instructors developing new sections of ENGL 1101 and for researchers studying composition pedagogy. It demonstrates how OER adoption has been integrated into first-year writing programs at GGC.
subject: English Composition

title: Art Appreciation and Visual Culture Syllabus
content:
This syllabus outlines the Georgia Gwinnett College ARTS 1100 course, which introduces students to the visual arts through historical, cultural, and formal analysis. The course covers major art movements, media and techniques, and the role of art in diverse societies from prehistory to the digital age.

Assignments include museum visit reports, visual analysis essays, a collaborative art project, and a final portfolio. The syllabus incorporates open-access image databases and virtual museum tours as alternatives to expensive art history textbooks.

The course emphasizes inclusive representation in art history, featuring works by artists from underrepresented backgrounds. Learning outcomes are aligned with the USG Area C humanities requirement and Georgia Gwinnett College's general education goals.
subject: Art
